text: The surgeon navigates around the proximal small intestine, identifying the distribution of the mesenteric tumor. Using a vessel sealer, they carefully dissect through the mesentery, scoring the visceral peritoneum to thin it out and ensure precise vessel sealing. The dissection progresses in a V-shaped manner towards the root of the mesentery.

The surgeon continues dissecting the proximal mesentery near the mesenteric tumor, encountering some bleeding. The mesentery is flipped over to assess the area below the tumor. The surgeon scores the distal aspect of the mesentery and aligns it with the small bowel margin distal to the tumor. The plan is to resect the mesentery in block with the tumor, including the associated small bowel.

The surgeon slows down the procedure to focus on dissecting thick mesentery beneath the tumor using a vessel sealer. Venous bleeding occurs from the mesentery, and the surgeon applies pressure with the left hand while using robotic arms to manage the bleed. A silk suture is placed to control the bleeding, and a figure-of-eight suture is used to cinch the vessel closed.

The surgeon removes a stitch and cleans the surgical field, preparing to continue the mesenteric resection using a vessel sealer. The distal margin is checked for perfusion, appearing satisfactory. The surgeon uses blue loads to transect the bowel and white loads for the anastomosis. The specimen is moved to the right side of the abdomen, and the proximal margin is identified. A smudge on the camera lens is visible throughout the operation.

The surgeon evaluates the perfusion of the proximal small bowel using ICG, noting its gray appearance compared to the distal bowel. Due to poor perfusion, additional resection of the proximal margin is performed. The surgeon creates an antiparastaltic side-to-side anastomosis using a 60mm white load, followed by a second isoparastaltic anastomosis. The ICG shows strong perfusion in the distal bowel, while the proximal bowel appears less vibrant, prompting careful consideration of further resection.

The surgeon inspects the mesentery for any signs of bleeding, confirming it looks clear. The operation is nearing completion, with the small bowel being examined for remaining length. Upon inspection, a small carcinoid tumor is discovered approximately thirty centimeters proximal to the anastomosis. The surgeon identifies no pathological lymph nodes or masses in the surrounding mesentery and decides to perform a limited small bowel resection, followed by an isoparastaltic side-to-side anastomosis.
